message.content:
The salt spray had long since bleached Elias’s skin to the color of old parchment, and the rhythm of the waves had become the only clock he trusted. For twenty years, he had tended the Needle, a jagged spire of granite jutting out from the edge of the Blackwood Coast. He liked it that way. The sea was predictable; people were not.

It was a Tuesday, or perhaps a Wednesday—the days bled together in the gray mist—when the bottle washed up against the base of the lighthouse stairs. It wasn’t a pristine glass vessel from a children’s storybook. It was a jagged shard of a green beer bottle, the neck snapped off, sealed with a thick, tar-like substance that smelled of ozone and something metallic.

Elias frowned. He picked it up with a gloved hand. It was heavy, unnaturally so. He carried it up the spiral stairs, the iron groaning under his boots, and placed it on the lantern room table, next to the logbook.

He should have thrown it back. He should have ignored it. But the tar seal was cracked, revealing a sliver of paper inside. Curiosity, a feeling he thought he had extinguished decades ago, prickled at the back of his neck.

He used his pocketknife to pry the glass shard away. The paper inside was not paper at all, but a thin sheet of synthetic material, waterproof and durable. It unfolded with a crisp snap.

There was no handwriting. No plea for help. No love letter.

There was only a single line of text, printed in a font that looked like code, and a set of coordinates.

> **THE LIGHT IS A LIE. LOOK DOWN.**

Elias laughed, a dry, rasping sound. "Look down?" he muttered to the empty room. "At the rocks
